subject: Peter Lohmann's Newsletter - Issue #195
body:
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/5df6e4df-5362-4228-ac2e-f8368012e9c7/1.png?t=1731941557)
Follow image link: (https://peter.beehiiv.com/subscribe)
Caption: A no-fluff, twice-weekly publication for the property management industry.

## Reclaiming the Present Moment by Shunning my iPhone

About a month ago, I left my office to meet someone for lunch. Five minutes into the drive, I realized I had left my phone back at the office. There was no time to go back.

I knew how to get there without directions and had already confirmed with him that I would be there, so there wasnвЂ™t technically anything I _needed_ my phone for. But damned if I didnвЂ™t feel like I had left a limb back on my desk.

Those two hours without my phone felt incredibly unsettling. I felt aimless, anxious, and disconnected. Then as I acknowledged those feelings and realized how silly it was to be feeling that way after being without a small glass rectangle for two hours, I became extremely unsettled. Did that explain why I rarely felt _truly_ present anymore?

IвЂ™ve struggled for years to feel present in large swaths of day-to-day life. As a business owner itвЂ™s very difficult вЂњturn it offвЂќ when work is over for the day. The hyper-connected always-available work culture of modern-day America makes all that worse. Then throw an internet-connected dopamine machine into your pocket, and itвЂ™s a total disaster for oneвЂ™s ability to focus, be present, and actually relax.

I really thought I was doing this right, because IвЂ™ve had notifications turned off for everything except calls and texts for over a decade! Not only that, but years ago I deleted the worst of the addictive apps (Twitter, Insta, Facebook and TikTok). And IвЂ™ve been on a total news blackout for over a year. Nevertheless I was reaching for my phone every couple of minutes or so for MOST OF MY WAKING HOURS. Stopping the incoming beeps and boops wasnвЂ™t enough. I would just refresh Slack. Check LinkedIn. Look at texts. Delete spam voicemail. Check email. Clear my unread call log. Repeat. Clearly aggressive action was needed.

**So IвЂ™m fighting back**. After a couple weeks of reflecting on that experience of leaving my phone behind, I decided it was time to sever the absurd connection my brain had built to an Apple iPhone. After a bunch of experimentation, I landed on two high-impact but straightforward changes that have cut my casual phone use by more than half (so far):

1. Created a new вЂњFocusвЂќ mode that is on 24/7. Set to вЂњOnly allow calls from contacts.вЂќ That stops ALL the spam calls. вЂњSilence unknown callersвЂќ is garbage by the way. You end up with notifications to clear and red badges on your Phone icon. To that Focus mode, I added short hand-picked list of people whose text messages would actually punch through and notify me.

2. Phone goes IN A DRAWER when IвЂ™m at home. Drawers are impulse kryptonite. They both hide it from your view, and force you to take a physical action to access the phone. It really makes your brain stop and think вЂњDo I REALLY need to look at my phone right now?вЂќ

So thatвЂ™s where I am so far (this is a work in progress.)

IвЂ™d like to put even more distance between myself and my phone, but if you try to do this, youвЂ™ll quickly realize some issues. First, most people need to be reachable in case of emergency. Especially parents. The destruction of the office lines and home phones (everyone just uses their cell now) has really made this challenging. One possible solution is a cellular-connected, locked-down apple watch. IвЂ™ve yet to try this though. Second, you donвЂ™t realize how much you use (and need) your phone. Think: GPS, 2FA, etc. YouвЂ™re CONSTANTLY forced to reach for it just to handle basic life tasks. ThatвЂ™s why the Focus mode is needed; at times you just really need your phone at hand.

Basically, IвЂ™m sick of my phone. I donвЂ™t want it anymore. I want to enjoy the people IвЂ™m with, and if IвЂ™m alone, I want to enjoy the solitude and be able to fully immerse myself in whatever IвЂ™m doingвЂ”even if thatвЂ™s doing nothing at all.

-Peter

----------## The PM Insurance Model No One Talks About

I've owned rental property since 2008 and never once questioned whether there was an alternative to just writing a check to a traditional insurance carrier every year. Turns out there is, and the big multifamily operators have known about it for a while.

This weekвЂ™s podcast is with Nicolas Lares of Insur3Tech, and we go deep on captive insurance - a structure where policyholders co-own the insurance company and share in the underwriting profit. 

Youtube: The PM Insurance Model No One Talks About (But Should) with Nicolas Lares | Peter Lohmann's Podcast (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HYOIAcgDfYM)

We get into how it works for scattered-site PM companies, what renters insurance captives actually look like in practice, and what questions you should be asking if you're shopping these programs.

_Also available on _[_Apple Podcasts_](https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/the-pm-insurance-model-no-one-talks-about-but-should/id1554806227?i=1000772177340)_ and _[_Spotify_](https://open.spotify.com/episode/7Gb4xbh2GE0lIcRoM8LU69?si=87dc718e7c3f41e9)
